judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are comparable in substance and correctly hit all three expectations with nearly identical depth and accuracy; Report 2 adds a small extra (WITH CHECK clause suggestion) while Report 1 adds a small extra (SET LOCAL pooled-connection hardening), but neither is required by the expectations.

report_1.evidence: cedar_owner is *both* a superuser and the table owner, so the tenant_isolation policy is never evaluated for it... unless the table is additionally marked ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY (which this schema does not do — confirmed by grep, no FORCE anywhere)

report_2.evidence: a table's owner and any superuser bypass row-level security. ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY alone is *not* enforced against the table owner — that requires FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, which the migration never issues; and a superuser bypasses RLS unconditionally regardless of FORCE. Because cedar_owner is both the superuser and the owning role, the tenant_isolation policy is silently inert for every query the app makes.

report_1.evidence: Point the application at the non-privileged role. Change DATABASE_URL in .env (and .env.example) from cedar_owner to cedar_app... Structurally going forward: keep a clear two-role split — cedar_owner (privileged; migrations, DDL, seeding only) and cedar_app (the runtime application identity, RLS-constrained). The app process should *only* ever hold cedar_app credentials.

report_2.evidence: Point the application at cedar_app, not cedar_owner. Change DATABASE_URL in cedarline/.env and .env.example to postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar... Keep cedar_owner for migrations only. scripts/migrate.sh and the one-time bootstrap should continue to run as the schema-owning cedar_owner; nothing the running app does should use it. This is the two-role model: one owner role for DDL/migrations, one least-privilege role for the app.

report_1.evidence: The cedar_app role is created *only* by the manual, one-time bootstrap db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, whose own header states it is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup." Nothing in the migrations (migrate.sh → db/migrations/*.sql) creates it... So a wipe-and-rebuild throws away cedar_app (it lived in the deleted volume) and never recreates it — the reset re-runs migrations but skips the one-time role bootstrap

report_2.evidence: The cedar_app role is a cluster-level object created only by the one-time manual bootstrap db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql (its own comment: "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup.")... Nothing in the reset re-runs the bootstrap, and docs/local-setup.md reinforces the gap by describing reset as only "Re-run migrations."
